questions.question: What is the difference between INNER JOIN, LEFT JOIN, RIGHT JOIN, and FULL OUTER JOIN?
questions.answer:
JOIN types control which rows survive when two tables are combined:

  • INNER JOIN — only rows where the condition matches in both tables. Most common in day-to-day queries.
  • LEFT JOIN — all rows from the left table + matching rows from the right. Non-matching right rows become NULL. I use this when I need "all orders including those with no payment yet".
  • RIGHT JOIN — mirror of LEFT JOIN. Rarely write this; I just flip the table order and use LEFT JOIN for clarity.
  • FULL OUTER JOIN — all rows from both sides, NULLs where there's no match. Useful for reconciliation queries — "show me everything on both sides that doesn't match".

In FolioTrack I had a LEFT JOIN between Portfolios and PortfolioHoldings to list all portfolios even if they had zero holdings yet — INNER JOIN would have excluded empty portfolios.
followUps: What's a CROSS JOIN?
When would you use a self-join?
How does a JOIN affect query performance?
questions.tip: Interview gotcha: If asked "what's the default JOIN?", the answer is INNER JOIN. Also be ready to sketch a Venn diagram — interviewers love that.

questions.question: What is the difference between WHERE and HAVING?
questions.answer:
Simple rule I follow: WHERE filters rows before grouping, HAVING filters groups after grouping.

WHERE works on individual row data — you can't use aggregate functions like SUM() or COUNT() in a WHERE clause. HAVING works on the result of a GROUP BY and can reference aggregates.

-- WHERE: filter rows before grouping
SELECT CategoryId, SUM(Amount) AS Total
FROM Orders
WHERE Status = 'Completed'  -- filters individual rows
GROUP BY CategoryId
HAVING SUM(Amount) > 10000; -- filters aggregated groups

Real example: in the PaymentReminder service I needed "categories that have more than 5 overdue invoices". That's a HAVING query — you can't know the count until after grouping.
followUps: Can you use column alias in HAVING?
What's the execution order of SQL clauses?
questions.tip: SQL execution order: FROM → JOIN → WHERE → GROUP BY → HAVING → SELECT → ORDER BY → LIMIT. Memorise this — it explains why you can't use SELECT aliases in WHERE.

questions.question: What is an index and how does it improve query performance?
questions.answer:
An index is a separate data structure (typically a B-tree) that SQL Server maintains alongside a table. Instead of scanning every row (full table scan), the engine can jump to the matching rows using the index — like a book index vs reading every page.

Clustered index — determines the physical storage order of the table. One per table. By default, SQL Server creates it on the primary key.

Non-clustered index — a separate structure with a pointer back to the data row. You can have many per table. Add these on columns you filter or join on frequently.

In the CSInvoice project, a query on Invoices WHERE ClientId = @id AND Status = 'Pending' was doing a full scan of 200k rows. Adding a composite non-clustered index on (ClientId, Status) dropped it from 3 seconds to 40ms.

Trade-off: Indexes speed up reads but slow down writes (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE have to update the index too). Don't over-index — only add indexes for actual slow queries.

projectExample.explanation: Composite index on filter columns; INCLUDE adds frequently SELECTed columns as covering index to avoid key lookups.

followUps: What is a covering index?
What is index fragmentation?
When would you drop an index?
questions.tip: Covering index = index includes all columns the query needs so SQL Server never has to go back to the table (eliminates key lookup). This is the single biggest performance win I've gotten in production.

questions.question: What is a stored procedure and what are its advantages over inline SQL?
questions.answer:
A stored procedure is a named, precompiled block of SQL stored in the database. You call it by name with parameters rather than sending raw SQL from application code.

Advantages:

  • Execution plan caching — SQL Server compiles and caches the plan on first call. Subsequent calls reuse it, avoiding recompilation overhead.
  • Security — you can grant EXECUTE permission on the proc without exposing the underlying tables at all.
  • Network efficiency — send one short EXEC command instead of a multi-KB SQL string.
  • Centralised logic — business rules live in DB, consistent regardless of which app calls it.

Disadvantages: Hard to version control, logic scattered between app and DB, painful to unit test. In the offline invoice project we had a stored proc for the nightly reconciliation job (Dapper + CommandType.StoredProcedure) but all read queries stayed as parameterized SQL in the application.
followUps: What is sp_executesql?
What is parameter sniffing?
Stored procedure vs view vs function?
questions.tip: Parameter sniffing — SQL Server optimises the execution plan for the first parameter values it sees. If you later call with very different values (e.g., a customer with 1 order vs 100,000 orders), the cached plan may be terrible. Fix: OPTION(RECOMPILE) or OPTIMIZE FOR UNKNOWN.

questions.question: What are SQL transactions and what are ACID properties?
questions.answer:
A transaction is a unit of work that either fully completes or fully rolls back — no partial state. ACID is the guarantee set:

  • Atomicity — all operations succeed or all roll back. Transfer ₹500: debit one account AND credit another as one atomic unit.
  • Consistency — DB moves from one valid state to another. Constraints, foreign keys, triggers all enforce this.
  • Isolation — concurrent transactions don't see each other's partial work. Controlled by isolation level.
  • Durability — committed data survives crashes. SQL Server writes to transaction log first (write-ahead logging).

In the WalletVerification project, a user's wallet top-up involved inserting a WalletTransaction record AND updating the Wallet.Balance. Without a transaction, a crash mid-way would debit the user without crediting the balance. We wrapped both in BEGIN TRANSACTION...COMMIT (and in EF Core via dbContext.Database.BeginTransactionAsync()).
followUps: What are SQL isolation levels?
What is a deadlock?
Explain dirty read, phantom read, non-repeatable read
questions.tip: Isolation levels ranked low→high: READ UNCOMMITTED → READ COMMITTED (SQL Server default) → REPEATABLE READ → SERIALIZABLE. Higher isolation = fewer anomalies but more locking/blocking. SNAPSHOT isolation is a great middle ground — MVCC-based, no read locks.

questions.question: What are SQL isolation levels and what concurrency problems do they solve?
questions.answer:
Isolation levels control what a transaction can see from other in-progress transactions. The three anomalies they protect against:

  • Dirty read — reading uncommitted data from another transaction that may roll back.
  • Non-repeatable read — re-reading the same row in the same transaction and getting a different value because another transaction committed in between.
  • Phantom read — re-running a range query and seeing new rows appear (another transaction inserted and committed).
LevelDirty ReadNon-RepeatablePhantom
READ UNCOMMITTED✓ possible✓✓
READ COMMITTED✗ prevented✓✓
REPEATABLE READ✗✗✓
SERIALIZABLE✗✗✗

In WalletVerification we explicitly used READ COMMITTED (SQL Server default) for most queries and SERIALIZABLE only for the debit/credit pair to prevent double-spend.
followUps: What is SNAPSHOT isolation?
How does SQL Server implement READ COMMITTED SNAPSHOT?
questions.tip: SNAPSHOT isolation (MVCC) lets readers not block writers and vice versa. Enable with ALTER DATABASE SET READ_COMMITTED_SNAPSHOT ON. Azure SQL has it on by default. This eliminates most blocking in OLTP systems.

questions.question: What is normalisation and what are the normal forms (1NF, 2NF, 3NF)?
questions.answer:
Normalisation is the process of structuring a database to reduce data redundancy and improve integrity.

  • 1NF (First Normal Form) — each column holds atomic (single) values; no repeating groups. No array-of-emails in one cell.
  • 2NF (Second Normal Form) — must be in 1NF + every non-key column depends on the whole primary key (no partial dependency). Applies when you have composite PKs.
  • 3NF (Third Normal Form) — must be in 2NF + no transitive dependency (non-key column depends only on the PK, not on another non-key column). E.g., storing PostalCode AND City when City is determined by PostalCode — extract to a PostalCodes table.

In FolioTrack I had a denormalized Holdings table that stored StockSymbol and StockName together. When a stock name changed I had to update thousands of rows. Splitting into a Stocks table (3NF) fixed it.
followUps: What is BCNF?
When would you deliberately denormalize?
questions.tip: Denormalization is valid for read-heavy OLAP/reporting scenarios — the join overhead of fully normalized tables can kill performance at scale. Data warehouses intentionally use star/snowflake schemas which are denormalized.

questions.question: What is a deadlock in SQL and how do you prevent it?
questions.answer:
A deadlock happens when two transactions each hold a lock the other needs and both wait forever. SQL Server detects it, picks a victim (lowest cost to roll back), kills that transaction, and throws error 1205.

Classic pattern: Transaction A locks Table1 then wants Table2. Transaction B locked Table2 and wants Table1. Neither can proceed.

Prevention strategies:

  • Access tables in the same order across all transactions — breaks the circular wait.
  • Keep transactions short — acquire locks late, release early.
  • Use lower isolation levels — READ COMMITTED SNAPSHOT (MVCC) eliminates most reader-writer deadlocks.
  • Index properly — full table scans lock more rows than necessary. A missing index caused a deadlock in the offline invoice project because the scan locked the entire Invoices table.
  • Optimistic concurrency — rowversion/timestamp columns; detect conflict at commit rather than locking upfront (EF Core [ConcurrencyCheck]).
followUps: How do you detect which queries are causing deadlocks?
What is a lock timeout vs deadlock?
questions.tip: Deadlock graph in SQL Server Extended Events (or System Health session) shows exactly which statements deadlocked. Always check this before guessing — the actual cause is usually an index gap or a transaction that does application logic between DB calls.

questions.question: What is the difference between DELETE, TRUNCATE, and DROP?
questions.answer:
Three very different operations that beginners often confuse:

  • DELETE — DML statement. Removes specific rows (WHERE clause optional). Logged row-by-row, can be rolled back, fires DELETE triggers, doesn't reset identity counter. Slow on large tables.
  • TRUNCATE — DDL statement. Removes ALL rows from a table. Minimally logged (logs page deallocations, not individual rows), extremely fast, resets identity counter, does NOT fire DELETE triggers. Can be rolled back if inside an explicit transaction. Cannot be used if the table has FK references pointing to it.
  • DROP — DDL statement. Removes the entire table (structure + data + indexes + constraints). Irreversible unless you have a backup.

Rule of thumb: need to wipe a staging/temp table between runs? Use TRUNCATE. Need to remove specific records? DELETE with WHERE. Never mix them up in production.
followUps: Can TRUNCATE be rolled back?
Why can't you TRUNCATE a table with foreign key references?
questions.tip: TRUNCATE resets identity — if you truncate and re-insert, IDs start from 1 again. This breaks any hardcoded ID references elsewhere. Always double check.

questions.question: What are window functions in SQL? Give an example.
questions.answer:
Window functions compute values across a set of rows related to the current row — without collapsing them into a group. They're the SQL equivalent of "calculate something for each row but keep all rows visible".

Common window functions: ROW_NUMBER(), RANK(), DENSE_RANK(), LAG(), LEAD(), SUM() OVER(), AVG() OVER().

-- Rank invoices by amount within each client
SELECT
  ClientId,
  InvoiceId,
  Amount,
  ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY ClientId ORDER BY Amount DESC) AS RowNum,
  SUM(Amount) OVER (PARTITION BY ClientId) AS ClientTotal
FROM Invoices;

In the offline invoice project I used ROW_NUMBER() with PARTITION BY ClientId to find the most recent invoice per client — a classic "get latest row per group" pattern.
followUps: Difference between RANK() and DENSE_RANK()?
What is PARTITION BY vs GROUP BY?
questions.tip: ROW_NUMBER() trick for deduplication: wrap in a CTE, then DELETE WHERE RowNum > 1. This is the cleanest way to remove duplicate rows from a table.

questions.question: What is a CTE (Common Table Expression) and when would you use it?
questions.answer:
A CTE is a named temporary result set defined with a WITH clause that you can reference like a table in the query that follows. It doesn't persist after the query executes — it's just a readability/organisational tool.

WITH OverdueClients AS (
  SELECT ClientId, COUNT(*) AS OverdueCount
  FROM Invoices
  WHERE DueDate < GETDATE() AND Status != 'Paid'
  GROUP BY ClientId
  HAVING COUNT(*) > 3
)
SELECT c.Name, oc.OverdueCount
FROM Clients c
INNER JOIN OverdueClients oc ON c.Id = oc.ClientId
ORDER BY oc.OverdueCount DESC;

When to use CTEs:

  • Break a complex query into readable steps
  • Avoid repeating subquery logic
  • Recursive queries (org chart traversal, bill of materials)
  • The "get top N per group" pattern with ROW_NUMBER()

Important: a CTE is NOT a performance optimisation. SQL Server may materialise it or inline it — the optimizer decides. If you need guaranteed materialisation, use a temp table (#Table).
followUps: What is a recursive CTE?
CTE vs temp table vs subquery — when to use which?
questions.tip: Recursive CTE for hierarchical data (e.g., employee → manager chain): define an anchor member + recursive member. Set OPTION (MAXRECURSION n) to avoid infinite loops.

questions.question: What is query plan and how do you use EXPLAIN/execution plan to optimise a query?
questions.answer:
The execution plan (or query plan) is the set of steps SQL Server's optimizer chooses to execute a query. It shows what operations happen in what order — table scans, index seeks, hash joins, sort operations, etc.

In SQL Server: press Ctrl+M in SSMS (actual execution plan) or Ctrl+L (estimated). Look for:

  • Table scan — reading every row. Almost always needs an index.
  • Key lookup — found the row via index but had to go back to the table for extra columns. Fix: covering index with INCLUDE.
  • Hash join — memory-intensive. Better when there's a proper index for a nested loop or merge join.
  • Sort — expensive. Can be eliminated by an ordered index.
  • Fat arrows — thick arrows between operators mean lots of rows flowing through. That's where the cost is.

In the CSInvoice project a report query had 80% cost in a sort operator. Adding an index on the ORDER BY column eliminated the sort entirely — query went from 8s to 200ms.
followUps: What is a statistics blob?
How does the optimizer choose between an index seek and scan?
questions.tip: Statistics are the query optimizer's eyes — if stats are stale, it guesses row counts wrong and picks a bad plan. Run UPDATE STATISTICS TableName or enable AUTO_UPDATE_STATISTICS.

questions.question: What is the N+1 problem in database queries?
questions.answer:
The N+1 problem is when you execute 1 query to get N parent records, then fire 1 query per parent to get its children — totalling N+1 queries instead of 1.

// BAD — N+1 with EF Core
var orders = await _db.Orders.ToListAsync();          // 1 query
foreach (var o in orders)
{
    var items = await _db.OrderItems                   // N queries!
        .Where(i => i.OrderId == o.Id)
        .ToListAsync();
}

// GOOD — single query with Include
var orders = await _db.Orders
    .Include(o => o.OrderItems)
    .ToListAsync();  // 1 query with JOIN

In FolioTrack I caught this when profiling with EF Core logging — loading 50 portfolios was firing 51 queries. Added .Include(p => p.Holdings) and it became 1 query. EF Core's AsSplitQuery() is another option for very wide result sets.
followUps: What is AsSplitQuery() in EF Core?
How do you detect N+1 in production?
questions.tip: Enable EF Core SQL logging in development: optionsBuilder.LogTo(Console.WriteLine, LogLevel.Information). If you see the same query firing in a loop, you have N+1. MiniProfiler is excellent for this in staging.

questions.question: What is the difference between clustered and non-clustered indexes?
questions.answer:
Clustered index — the table's data is physically sorted and stored in this index's order. Only ONE per table. SQL Server creates it on the primary key by default. Think of it as the table itself organised as a B-tree.

Non-clustered index — a separate structure that stores the index key columns + a pointer (row locator) back to the actual data row. You can have up to 999 per table in SQL Server. Think of it as a book index at the back.

Key implications:

  • Clustered index seeks are faster for range scans because data is physically ordered.
  • Non-clustered index requires an extra hop (key lookup) to get columns not in the index — unless it's a covering index.
  • Inserting rows in random order into a clustered index causes page splits — that's why GUIDs as PKs are problematic. Use sequential IDs (NEWSEQUENTIALID()) or integer identity.

In the CSInvoice DB, switching from GUID PKs to INT IDENTITY for the Invoices table reduced index fragmentation from 70% to under 5% and improved insert throughput by 40%.
followUps: What is index fragmentation?
Why are GUIDs bad as clustered index keys?
What is FILL FACTOR?
questions.tip: GUID clustered index problem: random GUIDs insert into the middle of the B-tree causing constant page splits and high fragmentation. Use NEWSEQUENTIALID() or a sequential integer for clustered index columns.

questions.question: What is a view in SQL and when would you use it?
questions.answer:
A view is a saved SELECT query that acts like a virtual table. The view definition is stored in the database; when you query the view, SQL Server executes the underlying SELECT transparently.

Use cases:

  • Simplify complex joins — expose a joined result as a simple table for reporting queries.
  • Security — grant users access to the view without exposing underlying tables (mask sensitive columns like salary, SSN).
  • Backward compatibility — when you rename/restructure tables, keep the old view name pointing to the new structure so existing queries don't break.

Indexed (materialised) view — SQL Server can create a clustered index on a view, making it physically stored and auto-maintained. Huge performance win for aggregate reporting queries.

In the offline invoice project I created a vw_ClientInvoiceSummary view joining Clients, Invoices, and Payments — the reporting module queried this view instead of writing the same 4-table join everywhere.
followUps: What is an indexed/materialised view?
Can you UPDATE through a view?
View vs stored procedure — when to use which?
questions.tip: Views are NOT performance optimisations by default — SQL Server inlines the view definition into your query. The optimizer sees the same SQL whether you use a view or write the query directly. Only indexed views materialise data.

questions.question: How does SQL Server handle concurrency? Explain optimistic vs pessimistic concurrency.
questions.answer:
Pessimistic concurrency — lock data when you read it, prevent others from modifying it until you're done. SQL Server's default lock-based approach. Safe but causes blocking under heavy concurrent load.

Optimistic concurrency — don't lock at read time. At commit time, check if data changed since you read it. If changed, reject the update (conflict). Works well when conflicts are rare.

In SQL Server: Optimistic concurrency is implemented via rowversion (or timestamp) columns. When you read a row you capture its rowversion. At update time you include WHERE RowVersion = @capturedVersion — if it changed, 0 rows affected = conflict detected.

In EF Core: Use [Timestamp] attribute or .IsRowVersion() in fluent API. EF Core automatically throws DbUpdateConcurrencyException when 0 rows affected.

In WalletVerification we used optimistic concurrency on the Wallet entity to prevent double top-up from rapid button clicks. The second click would always fail the rowversion check and the user got a friendly retry message.
followUps: What is DbUpdateConcurrencyException and how do you handle it?
When to use pessimistic vs optimistic?
questions.tip: Pessimistic concurrency in EF Core: use dbContext.Database.ExecuteSqlRawAsync("SELECT ... WITH (UPDLOCK, ROWLOCK)") to hold a write lock. Useful for inventory decrement operations where you absolutely cannot allow two threads to read the same value simultaneously.

questions.question: What is a foreign key and how does it enforce referential integrity?
questions.answer:
A foreign key is a column (or set of columns) in one table that references the primary key of another table. SQL Server enforces that the referenced row actually exists — this is called referential integrity.

What SQL Server prevents:

  • Inserting a row in the child table referencing a non-existent parent.
  • Deleting a parent row that has child rows (unless you define ON DELETE CASCADE or ON DELETE SET NULL).
  • Updating the parent PK to a value that orphans children.
ALTER TABLE Invoices
ADD CONSTRAINT FK_Invoices_Clients
FOREIGN KEY (ClientId) REFERENCES Clients(Id)
ON DELETE RESTRICT  -- block delete if invoices exist
ON UPDATE CASCADE;  -- propagate PK changes

Performance note: Foreign keys implicitly create an index requirement on the referencing column (child side). SQL Server doesn't auto-create this index — you should add one manually to prevent full scans during cascade operations.
followUps: What is ON DELETE CASCADE vs ON DELETE SET NULL?
Can you temporarily disable FK constraints?
questions.tip: Always index the FK column in the child table. Without it, any delete from the parent causes a full table scan of the child to check for references. SQL Server warns you in the execution plan but won't create the index for you.

questions.question: What is database sharding and when would you use it?
questions.answer:
Sharding is horizontal partitioning — splitting a large database table across multiple physical databases (shards) based on a shard key. Each shard holds a subset of the rows.

Example: 100M user records split across 10 shards by UserId % 10. User ID 42 goes to shard 2, user ID 103 goes to shard 3.

When to consider sharding:

  • Single server can no longer handle the write throughput
  • Table is too large for indexes to fit in memory
    • Typically when you're past 1-5TB on a single server

Problems sharding introduces:

  • Cross-shard joins become application-level joins (expensive)
  • Transactions across shards require distributed transaction protocols
  • Rebalancing when you add a shard is painful
  • Queries not filtered by shard key must hit ALL shards (scatter-gather)

I haven't implemented sharding in my projects — at scale for FolioTrack and the invoice systems we were well within single-server limits. I'd exhaust vertical scaling and read replicas before sharding.
followUps: What is consistent hashing and why is it used for sharding?
How does Azure SQL Elastic Pool relate to sharding?
questions.tip: Sharding is often premature optimisation. Most apps never need it. First try: better indexes, query optimization, read replicas, caching, table partitioning (SQL Server built-in). Sharding adds enormous operational complexity.

questions.question: Write a SQL query to find the second highest salary from an Employee table.
questions.answer:
Classic interview question with multiple valid approaches:

-- Method 1: Using subquery (works everywhere)
SELECT MAX(Salary) AS SecondHighest
FROM Employee
WHERE Salary < (SELECT MAX(Salary) FROM Employee);

-- Method 2: Using OFFSET-FETCH (SQL Server 2012+, cleanest)
SELECT DISTINCT Salary
FROM Employee
ORDER BY Salary DESC
OFFSET 1 ROWS FETCH NEXT 1 ROWS ONLY;

-- Method 3: Using DENSE_RANK() (handles ties correctly)
SELECT Salary
FROM (
  SELECT Salary, DENSE_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY Salary DESC) AS Rnk
  FROM Employee
) ranked
WHERE Rnk = 2;

Why DENSE_RANK is best: If two employees share the highest salary, MAX(salary) WHERE salary < MAX correctly finds the second distinct value. But DENSE_RANK handles the generalised "Nth highest" case cleanly — just change WHERE Rnk = 2 to WHERE Rnk = N.
followUps: Write it for Nth highest salary
What if there are duplicate salaries?
questions.tip: Always clarify: second highest distinct value or second highest row? If two rows have salary 100000 (the max), is 100000 still the "second highest"? DENSE_RANK gives you distinct-value ranking; ROW_NUMBER gives you row ranking.

questions.question: What are SQL triggers and when should you use them?
questions.answer:
A trigger is a special stored procedure that automatically executes in response to INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE events on a table (DML triggers) or DDL events (schema changes).

DML trigger types:

  • AFTER trigger — fires after the DML completes. Can still rollback. Most common use: audit logging.
  • INSTEAD OF trigger — fires instead of the DML. Common use: making non-updatable views updatable.
-- Audit trigger: log every invoice status change
CREATE TRIGGER trg_Invoice_StatusChange
ON Invoices AFTER UPDATE
AS
BEGIN
  IF UPDATE(Status)
    INSERT INTO InvoiceAudit (InvoiceId, OldStatus, NewStatus, ChangedAt)
    SELECT d.Id, d.Status, i.Status, GETUTCDATE()
    FROM inserted i
    INNER JOIN deleted d ON i.Id = d.Id;
END;

When NOT to use triggers: Application logic in triggers is invisible to developers, hard to debug, can't be unit tested, fire for every row (performance at scale). I prefer application-level audit interceptors in EF Core over DB triggers for most cases.
followUps: What are the inserted and deleted pseudo-tables in triggers?
Can a trigger call another trigger (nested triggers)?
questions.tip: EF Core alternative to audit triggers: Override SaveChangesAsync() in DbContext and inspect ChangeTracker.Entries() — same audit capability but fully testable, debuggable, and under version control.

questions.question: What is the difference between UNION and UNION ALL?
questions.answer:
Both combine result sets from multiple SELECT statements, but they handle duplicates differently:

  • UNION — removes duplicate rows from the combined result. Requires an implicit DISTINCT operation which has a sorting/hashing cost.
  • UNION ALL — keeps ALL rows including duplicates. No deduplication step — faster.
-- UNION: removes duplicates (shared customers appear once)
SELECT CustomerId FROM OrdersArchive
UNION
SELECT CustomerId FROM OrdersCurrent;

-- UNION ALL: keeps duplicates (faster when duplicates OK)
SELECT CustomerId FROM OrdersArchive
UNION ALL
SELECT CustomerId FROM OrdersCurrent;

Rule: use UNION ALL by default unless you specifically need deduplication. The extra DISTINCT pass in UNION can be very expensive on large result sets.
followUps: What requirements must both SELECT statements meet for UNION?
Can you ORDER BY in a UNION query?
questions.tip: Column count and data types must match in both SELECT statements. The column names come from the first SELECT. You can ORDER BY only at the end of the entire UNION, not per-SELECT.

questions.question: How do you handle pagination in SQL?
questions.answer:
Two common approaches, each with trade-offs:

OFFSET-FETCH (SQL Server 2012+):

SELECT Id, Title, CreatedAt
FROM Articles
ORDER BY CreatedAt DESC
OFFSET (@Page - 1) * @PageSize ROWS
FETCH NEXT @PageSize ROWS ONLY;

Keyset pagination (cursor-based, better for large tables):

-- First page: no filter
SELECT TOP (@PageSize) Id, CreatedAt FROM Articles ORDER BY CreatedAt DESC;

-- Next pages: use last seen value as cursor
SELECT TOP (@PageSize) Id, CreatedAt
FROM Articles
WHERE CreatedAt < @LastSeenCreatedAt
ORDER BY CreatedAt DESC;

Why keyset is better at scale: OFFSET scans and discards all preceding rows — page 1000 with 20-row pages means scanning 19,980 rows to throw them away. Keyset jumps directly to the cursor position using an index seek.

In FolioTrack's news feed I used keyset pagination on the Angular client with a ?cursor= query param — no visible degradation at any page depth.
followUps: What are the limitations of keyset pagination?
How do you get total count efficiently with pagination?
questions.tip: Avoid COUNT(*) for total pages on large tables — it's a full scan. Consider caching the count, accepting approximate counts, or using filtered indexes if total count is essential.

questions.question: What is the difference between a temp table and a table variable?
questions.answer:
Both store temporary data during a query session but they behave differently:

Feature#TempTable@TableVariable
ScopeSession / stored procBatch / current scope
Transaction-awareYes (rolls back)No (survives rollback)
StatisticsYes (optimizer uses real stats)No (assumes 1 row)
IndexesFull index supportOnly PK/UNIQUE
Tempdb usageAlways in tempdbIn tempdb for large sets, memory for small
Best forLarger result sets, complex processingSmall result sets (<100 rows), simple passing of data

Critical insight: Table variables tell the optimizer they have exactly 1 row (hardcoded estimate). If you put 50,000 rows in a table variable and join on it, the optimizer picks a catastrophically bad plan. For anything non-trivial, use a temp table so the optimizer gets real statistics.
followUps: What is tempdb? Why is it a bottleneck?
What is a table-valued parameter?
questions.tip: When joining a large table variable, the optimizer assumes 1 row and picks nested loops. This causes performance disasters. Always use #TempTable for intermediate result sets that will be joined.

questions.question: What is SQL injection and how do you prevent it?
questions.answer:
SQL injection is when attacker-controlled input is interpreted as SQL commands instead of data. Classic example:

-- Vulnerable: string concatenation
var query = $"SELECT * FROM Users WHERE Name = '{userInput}'";
-- Input: ' OR 1=1 --
-- Resulting SQL: SELECT * FROM Users WHERE Name = '' OR 1=1 --'
-- Returns ALL users!

Prevention — always use parameterized queries:

// Dapper (offline invoice project)
var user = await _db.QueryFirstOrDefaultAsync<User>(
    "SELECT * FROM Users WHERE Name = @name",
    new { name = userInput });  // @name is a parameter, never concatenated

// EF Core — always parameterized automatically
var user = await _db.Users
    .Where(u => u.Name == userInput)  // LINQ translates to @p0 parameter
    .FirstOrDefaultAsync();

// Raw SQL in EF Core — use FromSqlInterpolated NOT FromSqlRaw with concat
var users = _db.Users.FromSqlInterpolated($"SELECT * FROM Users WHERE Name = {userInput}");

I've never had an injection vulnerability because I've never concatenated user input into SQL — always parameters or EF Core LINQ.
followUps: What is stored procedure injection?
How does parameterization prevent injection at the protocol level?
questions.tip: EF Core's FromSqlRaw is dangerous if used with string concatenation. Use FromSqlInterpolated (which safely parameterizes interpolated strings) or FromSqlRaw with explicit parameters only.

questions.question: What is the difference between primary key and unique key?
questions.answer:
Both enforce uniqueness but they differ in several important ways:

  • Primary Key (PK): Cannot be NULL. Only one per table. SQL Server automatically creates a clustered index on it (by default). Uniquely identifies each row — it's the main entity identifier.
  • Unique Key (Unique Constraint): Allows one NULL (because NULL != NULL in SQL). Multiple unique constraints allowed per table. Creates a non-clustered index by default. Used for alternate keys — business keys that must be unique but aren't the main identifier.

Example: in the Clients table, Id (INT IDENTITY) is the PK. Email has a UNIQUE constraint — it must be unique per client but it's not the clustered index because it's variable length and changes occasionally (people update emails).
followUps: Can a primary key be a composite key?
Why is a sequential integer usually preferred over a GUID for PK?
questions.tip: NULLs in unique constraints: Most databases allow multiple NULLs in a unique column because NULL != NULL. SQL Server is consistent with this. If you want to prevent multiple NULLs, add a filtered unique index: CREATE UNIQUE INDEX ... WHERE Email IS NOT NULL.

questions.question: What is database partitioning in SQL Server?
questions.answer:
Table partitioning splits a large table into smaller physical storage units (partitions) based on a partition function and column value. From the application's perspective the table is still one logical table — SQL is unchanged.

Common strategy: partition by date (e.g., one partition per month). Old partitions rarely change and can be compressed or archived. New data hits only the current partition.

-- Partition function: split by year
CREATE PARTITION FUNCTION pfInvoiceYear (INT)
AS RANGE RIGHT FOR VALUES (2022, 2023, 2024, 2025);

-- Partition scheme maps partitions to filegroups
CREATE PARTITION SCHEME psInvoiceYear
AS PARTITION pfInvoiceYear ALL TO ([PRIMARY]);

-- Create partitioned table
CREATE TABLE Invoices (
  Id INT, InvoiceYear INT, Amount DECIMAL(18,2)
) ON psInvoiceYear(InvoiceYear);

Benefits: Partition elimination (queries filtered on partition column skip non-relevant partitions), faster archiving (partition switch = metadata operation, instant), better maintenance (rebuild index per partition).
followUps: What is partition elimination?
How is partitioning different from sharding?
questions.tip: Partitioning vs sharding: Partitioning splits data across filegroups within a single SQL Server instance. Sharding splits data across multiple server instances. Partitioning is transparent to the application; sharding requires application-level routing.

questions.question: What is a composite index and when should you use it?
questions.answer:
A composite (multi-column) index includes more than one column. The order of columns in the index definition matters critically:

  • SQL Server can use a composite index if the query filters by the leading column(s).
  • Filtering on column 2 alone (skipping column 1) causes an index scan, not seek — effectively ignoring the index.
-- Index on (ClientId, Status, InvoiceDate)
CREATE INDEX IX_Invoices_Composite ON Invoices (ClientId, Status, InvoiceDate);

-- Uses index efficiently (leading columns present)
WHERE ClientId = 5 AND Status = 'Pending'

-- Also uses index (leading column present)
WHERE ClientId = 5

-- Does NOT use index efficiently (skips ClientId)
WHERE Status = 'Pending'

Design rule — selectivity first: Put the most selective column first (fewest distinct values per row, e.g., ClientId). But also consider your most common query patterns — sometimes you put a less selective equality column first so range scans happen on the second column.

I used a composite index on (ClientId, DueDate) in the PaymentReminder service — the nightly job queried exactly that pattern every time.
followUps: What is the rule of thumb for column order in composite indexes?
What is an index with INCLUDE columns?
questions.tip: Think of composite indexes like a phone book: sorted by last name then first name. You can look up by last name alone (efficient), but looking up by first name alone means reading the whole book.

questions.question: What are aggregate functions in SQL? Give examples.
questions.answer:
Aggregate functions compute a single result from a set of rows. They're used with GROUP BY or across the entire table.

  • COUNT(*) — total row count. COUNT(column) ignores NULLs.
  • SUM(column) — total sum. NULLs ignored.
  • AVG(column) — average. NULLs ignored (denominator = non-null count, not total rows).
  • MIN(column) / MAX(column) — min/max value.
  • STRING_AGG(column, separator) — concatenate string values (SQL Server 2017+).
SELECT
  ClientId,
  COUNT(*) AS InvoiceCount,
  SUM(Amount) AS TotalBilled,
  AVG(Amount) AS AvgInvoice,
  MAX(DueDate) AS LatestDue,
  STRING_AGG(Status, ', ') AS AllStatuses
FROM Invoices
GROUP BY ClientId
HAVING COUNT(*) > 5;

NULL trap: AVG(Amount) divides by the count of non-null values. If 3 out of 10 rows have NULL Amount, AVG divides by 7, not 10 — potentially misleading.
followUps: How does COUNT(*) differ from COUNT(1) and COUNT(column)?
What is GROUPING SETS?
questions.tip: COUNT(*) vs COUNT(column): COUNT(*) counts all rows including NULLs. COUNT(column) counts only non-NULL values in that column. These can give different numbers on the same table — always use COUNT(*) when you want total rows.

questions.question: Explain the SQL Server query execution pipeline.
questions.answer:
When you submit a query to SQL Server, it goes through these stages:

  1. Parsing — syntax check, builds a parse tree. Fails here if SQL is malformed.
  2. Binding (Algebrisation) — resolves object names (table names, column names) against the system catalog. Verifies the tables/columns exist.
  3. Query Optimisation — the optimizer considers multiple execution plans (join order, index choice, join type) and picks the lowest-cost one based on statistics. This is the most expensive step — plans are cached to avoid repeating it.
  4. Plan Cache Check — before optimising, SQL Server checks if a plan for this query already exists in the plan cache (matching parameter signature). If yes, skips optimization and reuses the plan.
  5. Execution — the execution engine runs the chosen plan, interacting with the buffer pool (memory) and reading from disk if needed (physical I/O).

Buffer pool (memory): SQL Server caches data pages in memory. Repeated queries on the same data avoid disk I/O. This is why "warming up" a SQL Server after restart helps — the buffer pool fills over time.
followUps: What is plan cache pollution?
What is the difference between logical and physical reads?
questions.tip: Logical reads vs physical reads: Logical = reads from buffer pool (memory, fast). Physical = reads from disk (slow). SET STATISTICS IO ON shows both. Tuning aims to reduce physical reads by ensuring the working set fits in memory.

questions.question: What is the difference between CHAR, VARCHAR, and NVARCHAR?
questions.answer:
String data type choice affects storage, performance, and internationalization:

  • CHAR(n) — fixed-length, always uses n bytes (right-padded with spaces). Best for fixed-length codes (country codes, status flags like 'Y'/'N'). Comparison is fast because length is always known.
  • VARCHAR(n) — variable-length, uses actual length + 2 bytes overhead. Best for variable-length ASCII/Latin text. More storage-efficient than CHAR for variable content.
  • NVARCHAR(n) — variable-length Unicode (UTF-16). Uses 2 bytes per character. Required for multilingual content (names in Hindi, Chinese, Arabic, emoji). NVARCHAR(MAX) can hold up to 2GB.

Performance: CHAR is slightly faster for comparison (fixed length). VARCHAR and NVARCHAR have similar performance. NVARCHAR uses 2x the storage of VARCHAR for the same content.

Rule: Use NVARCHAR for user-facing text (names, descriptions, messages). Use VARCHAR for internal codes/identifiers where Unicode isn't needed. Never use NVARCHAR just to be safe — the storage cost adds up on large tables.
followUps: When would you use VARCHAR(MAX)?
What is the difference between N'' and '' string literals?
questions.tip: Always prefix Unicode string literals with N: N'Abdul' not 'Abdul'. Without N, SQL Server uses the default collation (may corrupt non-ASCII characters). In parameterized queries this is handled automatically.
